sentences.text: It wasn't a definite identification.

Amongst one of the adults who came out, said that this must be [NON-ENGLISH] because his uniform was a very, very-- you could see that he is a very high in the force, which I can't call police force because it was not the police.
It was the gendarmes.
It was a different-- they were the executor for a long way back.
And I'm talking about 50 years back in the Hungarian history.
They were the police force in the country areas who where doing everything which was not, according to the Hungarians, not just by law, but by definition of the Hungarian way of life.
Which means they were anti-Jewish.
They were anti-communist.
They're anti-socialist.
They were anti-poor.
They were the henchmen of the landlords in Hungary, which landlords we're talking about thousands and thousands of acres per families.
And they were the executioners of them.
And they were feared all over Hungary, not just by the Jews, but by everybody who wasn't-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who wasn't wanted by the Hungarian regime.
And this guy was very, very high rank.
And this man in our wagon said that that must be [NON-ENGLISH].
And I recognized the man after when I went to the Hungarian Jewish because I was there when Szalasi, who was the head of the fascist movement in Hungary, was trialed and hanged.
And I was there when Imredy, who was the foreign minister in Hungary, during that time, he was trialed and was hanged.
And I was there when [NON-ENGLISH] who was the justice minister in the German times, was trialed and hanged.
The way I could go there, my father had a doctor friends who was the jail doctor.
And he could get tickets because the trials and the hanging was public.
And it happened in the Zeneakademia, which is conservatorium in Hungary.
And that's where trials took place in front of a people judge, with galleries full of people.
But you need a ticket to get there.
And when I came home from concentration camps, my father wanted me to go.
And he thought that my conscious would ease if these people come to justice.
Yeah.
From then on, from Kosice to Auschwitz, I cannot remember whether the door had been opened or not, or whether we got water or not.
Because the whole trip took, Thursday we left, Friday, Saturday, Sunday we arrived to Auschwitz.
So approximately 2 and 1/2 days we were on the train.
And during that time, people died in the wagon.
And we couldn't get rid of them.
We had to-- they had to stay.
The wagon was so packed that, if I was on one side of the wagon, which is not long-- it was about 10 meters, 11 meters.
I didn't know what's happening in the other end of the wagon.
Because, between me and the end of the wagon, another, I don't know how many, hundreds of people.
We were actually like sardines.
The door was locked.
Scream was heard.
We heard people talking absolutely out of ordinary, which mean we knew that they became mad on the trip.
And what I remember after that is, when they opened the wagon, and we came out to the platform, and it was a lot of people.
It's a lot of people.
It was like a tidal wave.
And we didn't know where we were.
And I never forget-- the train stops.
And we get off on the left-hand side on the platform.
And SS with dogs, uniforms, soldiers all around on the other side of the platform.
And all we heard is [SPEAKING GERMAN] which means, move, move, going.
And we started moving on the platform to a certain point, the top of the platform.
And you couldn't see much.
Only on the above, I saw the sign that 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' And there was no name of Auschwitz or anything.
We didn't know where we were.
That was day time.
But my mentality, even as a child, was shattered of the two days, two and a half days trip that I didn't know day, night.
All I knew, it was light because end of the line stood a man, a high officer, extremely good looking, extremely well dressed.
I remember his face, powder on him.
When a man shaved in those days, he put powder after the shaving.
He had a very vicious dog in his-- in his-- on the leash.
And everybody who walked have to walk in front of him.
He was standing there with a few other officers, smaller officer, but he was the head guy.
And I had these four, five friends of mine with me.
And this best friend of mine, Banja Ferica, he was smaller than I am.
And he was not as wise, streetwise, as I am.
And came the sign, which I told previously that, God gave me something.
I was well built.
I was a sportsman.
So I pulled my chest up.
I flexed my muscles in front of him.
Either I wanted to show that I am a man, or I don't know what.
And with his finger like this, he showed me, go to the right.
And Frank, my little mate, he had to go to the left.
And he looked at me.
And I said come with me, come on, in Hungarian.
Later on, we heard, in the lager, inside, that it was Dr.
Mengele, the head doctor of the lager.
And if you show-- you want me to draw his face today, I could.
And I was 14.
And that's 50-odd years already.
And then started my strange experience because I don't know that where they took us.
They took us into a bath section, which was a bath, a huge place with shower recess.
But it was not gas shower like the rest of them because I'm still alive.
But we had to undress.
And they took the clothes away from us and came adults, what we called [SPEAKING GERMAN], or prisoners, or whatever the name was-- who worked.
They were in kommandos.
We called them kommandos.
Later on, I learned, when I got into the Birkenau lager, that they were working.
They were shaving us, all the hair, the hair from the top, from your areas, underarms, they all been removed.
And they-- with a brush, they put some stuff on us which burned like hell, like something disinfectant.
And from then on-- no, first-- sorry-- first we had a shower after they took the hair off.
And after the shower, we got this disinfectant.
And then we go to a separate room.
And then we got the [SPEAKING GERMAN] uniforms, which was a stripe.
And we got shoes.
And I know the shoes I worked with when I was working.
But I'm not sure the shoes in the lager was the same.
But it was a funny shoes.
It had that a wood sole.
And it had a canvas top.
And the wood shoe was very high.
So it was very, very difficult to walk in them.
And I remember the shoes-- the reason I remember so well because after Auschwitz, I went to working in different lagers.
And I went with working kommandos.
Winter set in, and the snow stuck under the wooden shoes.
And they lead us through an area where I saw ovens.
At those time, I didn't know what the ovens for, or what is there.
And we pass the ovens.
And being a soccer player, I remember, because it was Sunday afternoon-- I know you asked me what time.
Now I know it was afternoon.
Because we passed an area, and it was a soccer game on.
And we saw the Germans guards, or, in those days, I only knew they are soldiers.
They played against-- could be the prisoners, or whoever it is.
But two teams was playing, and I, as a soccer player, I stopped and watched them.
And I said, this can't be a bad place where they are playing football.
And then I got the push.
And we passed that area.
And they took us into a lager.
And I have to say it in German because I don't know what the lager is in English.
And that was what they called Birkenau or, with the other word, the gypsy lager.
And I learned later on because I met relatives there-- later on I know what they called the gypsy lager because, in one side of a little creek, or whatever it was, it was our barracks.
And on the other side, it was the gypsy barracks, which meant that-- I don't know the number.
It could have been 10,000, 5,000 8,000.
A lot of gypsies were living.
And on the left hand side was our set of barracks.
I was in block 11 because that was the children barracks.
And the gypsy lager was something strange.
They were non-Jewish.
They were gypsies.
They came to us, to our side, and they were dealing with cigarettes because cigarettes was more than food, everything.
And then, little bread and whatever it was to make our life livable, or at least we ate something.
But we could not go over to their place.
And by the time when we arrived, the whole life of the barracks was organized.
Every lager had a-- every block had a block altester, which meant the elder, block elder, or whatever it is-- a Hungarian, or whatever.
And we lived in a barrack, where, if it's suitable for 200 people, it was 1,000.
It was suitable for 1,000, we were 4,000.
I don't know how many.
All we knew, we couldn't lie down.
We were sitting like this.
And the next guy was sitting against our leg.
And so, and so, and so.
One night, everybody into the barracks, big commotion.
And the barracks had windows-- but not windows, like in a cell.
And these taller people looked out.
And they said, something happening in the gypsy lager.
And we didn't know what's happening in the gypsy lager.
And next morning, we look over to the other side of the creek, or embankment, or something, and the gypsy lager was empty.
And by lunch time, we knew, because you could smell it, that all gypsies had been cremated by the Germans.
He said, you go in the back there.
I think-- so he's there.
So I went back, and I met this gentleman.
It will come during my speech.
The name will come back.
And he-- Dr.
Bela Fabian-- I told you it would come back.
This gentleman was a member of the Hungarian parliament and was a big somebody.
That's why I heard his name often mentioned in my home as a child.
And I called him Bela Bacsi, Uncle Bela.
And he was the first one who bought me a piece of bread with a little margarine, because in Germany was no butter, and a little marmalade.
And I ate after three and a half days, four days.
That was my first food.
He was somebody in there where they are distributing the food to the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And he bought me a piece of bread.
And I never forget.
And I said, thank you, Bela Bacsi.
That's my first food for about three days.
And then he bought me, whether water or some drink, I don't know.
And he said, you will stay in this block 11.
And you will get a little bit of better [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] distribution than the rest of them.
Now, we could go up and down.
It was 26, 27 block in our area.
Gypsies disappeared.
And I was walking.
And I heard, because in that place, was a lot of Hungarian, Polish, Lithuanian, I don't know other nationalities so well, whether it was French people or not.
I don't know.
I didn't--
And he was staying in block 24, 25.
He was already, those days, 45, 50-ish.
To me, he was a dead man.
I mean for a 13-years-old, a 50-years-old man was already a nonexistent old something.
And from then on, he started talking.
He asked me how is things at home.
And he was the brother of my grandmother, the youngest of my grandmother's side.
And he was the man who contributed-- who was the sign from him.
Because I was in Auschwitz for about, I can't tell you exactly, six weeks, two months.
Somewhere around August, he told me that, Jules-- or son, I can't remember-- you'd better get out of here because the crematorium is burning nonstop.
But during these two months, I had a lot of experience.
The toilets we set, because it was the only place where we could huddle together.
It started getting cold.
July, August, September, it started getting cold, particularly in Poland.
It's amongst mountains.
It's not like a Hungary, where July is still very hot.
In August, September is already cold.
And we started huddling together in the toilet and wherever because daytime, you couldn't stay in barracks.
You had to get out of the barracks.
And you just walked and dazed around.
Exactly the same.
Yeah, they are walking like on a promenade, down and up, and down and up, and that was it.
And because the food was-- we had, I don't know, 12 of us got a bread, a little marmalade, and a piece of butter.
And we got pumpkin.
I mentioned to you it will come back-- pumpkin soup.
And pumpkin soup was the menu for the rest of my German staying.
And the pumpkin soup had a tendency to give you a rush.
So the toilets were always full of people.
And the toilet was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] a deep.
They shoveled out.
They put a piece of timber.
And you just sit on the timber.
And the rest, it happened by nature.
That was the toilet.
And then I had-- I met a guy, who I didn't know who he was.
And then I met him in Hakoah Club 50 years later.
And his name was the Kleba Klein.
He run the car club.
And when I started playing in Hakoah he came to the match because he was a Hakoah supporter.
And he came to the dressing room one day, and he said, you look familiar.
Finally, we came to Germany somehow.
And then he said he was in block 11 in Auschwitz.
And then I know I met him.
And then I remembered him-- just as a guy, your young mind.
Fortunately, my mind is very vivid, because I can remember from Zarah Leander who played in-- and Paul Muni, when I was six, seven years of age.
And I remember films, and I can tell you this whole story what the film was about-- even my father couldn't remember.
And he said to me, Jules, they are coming here to collecting volunteers for work.
Get out of here.
And being my elder, and being my uncle, or whatever it is, my grandmother's brother is to me, one day came the Todt.
The Todt was the German working organization.
It was not a military, although everything worked for the military.
But it was a civil organization.
And they came to organize groups for some sort of a work.
And I volunteered.
And they got us together one day.
It's not very clear how.
They put us into, again, freight wagons, and we traveled one or two days.
And suddenly we had arrived to Germany, a place called [SPEAKING GERMAN] or [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That's the two names, as a child, I remember.
And that was a working camp for [SPEAKING GERMAN] industry, which was the largest German, I don't know, privately owned, or government owned, ammunition-making factories.
And the whole factory was underground in the woods.
Now, for me, it's very hard to describe it.
Simple reason, [SPEAKING GERMAN] had no way to know where are you, or what are you, or what you're doing, particularly when you are involved in ammunition stuff.
But we were in the middle of a forest, in a lager, which was-- seemed brand new, or practically new.
And they organized about 2,000 people, which was worse-- I was there about two months.
It was worse than Auschwitz, or any other lager I ever been.
Simple reason-- in two months, out of the 2,000, about 1,800 died.
And 1,800 died on the way to the work, on the job, on their way to come home, in the nighttime by the kapos and the block altesters.
Tortured, that we had to-- 2 o'clock, they wake us up, and we had to, naked and barefoot, had run on the concrete when the weather was already, let's say, two or three degrees.
The lice started eating us.
Many of them died of fleck typhus.
Unbelievable.
We were in barracks.
And that's where my knowledge of the shoes came, that we walked out to the jobs.
My job was, I don't know how far, in a stone quarry.
And the stone quarry was surrounded by work sheds.
And the stone gets crushed and then carried up by conveyor belt.
The conveyor belt was operated-- one of them was operated by a French guest worker.
He was not Jewish.
He speak very broken German.
And sometimes I got some little food from him.
And also, my life was bearable because I became a [SPEAKING GERMAN], one of the Wehrmacht soldiers, who was the guard, an elderly man, he got liked of me or something.
Whenever we arrived to the job, I could clean his shoes.
He brought me a piece of bread.
I got some extra soup.
I got a little bit of-- I could clean the food, what the German soldiers get who were guarding us.
And for that, I got the extra food.
And that kept me going.
But when you ask the sanitary things, the conditions were unbelievable.
I mean people died there.
We just got hold of them, put it on a hand carriage, whatever, and took them out there, somewhere, dig the hole and buried them.
And I was on that details for a while.
And when we got a piece of bread, I was sitting on a corpse and eating and then put the corpse into the graves and cover them up.
Sometimes.
Sometimes.
If the dead was too many, then we didn't have to go to the quarry.
But mostly, our job was in the quarry.
And inside there, the misery started after the jobs.
Because we had non-Jewish kapos and Jewish kapos-- who were just as cruel to us as the non-Jewish kapos.
The kapo is the one who looks after the inside.
Outside, we had wire fence, towers, reflectors-- I mean, lights.
Inside, whatever happened, that belonged to the block altesters and the kapos.
The block altesters were mostly elderly Jews.
The kapos were Jewish collaborators, murderers, or non-Jewish German homosexuals, criminals, and-- what others?
And politicals.
Sorry, and politicals.
They all had triangle in their striped uniform.
And different colors meant different-- if I'm not wrong, the black were the homosexuals.
The political were red.
And the homosexual were pink or red, something.
That was something.
I can't remember exactly.
But amongst us, we knew who were the-- and they killed us like flies.
People lost weight within two months, 15, 20, 25 kilos.
They couldn't let you sleep.
They beat you up with no reason whatsoever.
Sundays, where we didn't work, and we walked around outside, the went around with whips, and they whip up, just walking there.
Must have-- maybe the instruction was to make our life as miserable as possible.
Now, lice were eating us by the millions.
And when we worked outside the quarry, where they built a railroad, and they had cement sacks, paper.
And because we were freezing, we put paper bags underneath.
If they find you with paper bags, you get 25 on your ass with a stick, because they said you will get lice.
But we had already so many lice, when we walked, we didn't have to walk.
The lice was carrying us.
